nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to hide in sandy rubble, burrows, and crevices on coral reefs, often with only the head protruding. Depth range 1–67 m.

diet.notes: Dedicated piscivore in the wild, feeding primarily on small fish and occasionally crustaceans. In captivity, many specimens initially accept only live prey (small fish such as mollies or guppies); transitioning to frozen/thawed marine fish, silversides, shrimp, or squid can be attempted using a feeding stick and garlic-scented food. Refusal to eat is the leading cause of captive mortality and may persist despite varied offering strategies.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in the eel's mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Bernard DUPONT, taken at Wori, Manado, Sulawesi, Indonesia. 3190×2562 px. Shows adult male (blue phase). Explicit CC license confirmed on Commons page.
Photo by Brett, O.J. / tropicalfavourites.com. All rights reserved per FishBase gallery notice; recommend Wikimedia candidate instead.
Product page for Black Ribbon Eel at LiveAquaria; product photography is retailer-owned. Use only if no licensed alternative is acceptable.

summary: A striking expert-level moray eel of the Indo-Pacific, known for dramatic color changes tied to sex and age — juveniles are jet-black, adult males blue, and females yellow. Rarely survives long in captivity without expert husbandry.
careNotes:
SAME-SPECIES NOTE (sw-fish-020): Black Ribbon Eel and Blue Ribbon Eel are the same species (Rhinomuraena quaesita) at different life stages. Juveniles are jet-black with a yellow dorsal fin; adult males are vivid blue with yellow dorsal and anal fins; females (and older transformed males) are yellow. The reviewer should consider consolidating sw-fish-017 and sw-fish-020 into a single entry or clearly cross-referencing them.

Rhinomuraena quaesita is widely considered one of the most challenging marine fish to maintain in captivity and is recommended only for expert aquarists, public aquaria, or research institutions. The primary cause of captive mortality is food refusal — many wild-caught specimens cease eating entirely after capture and slowly starve. Captive survival of under one year is common; however, long-term keepers and public aquaria have reported specimens living up to 20 years under optimal conditions.

This species requires a deep sandy substrate (10–13 cm) in which to burrow, plus rock caves or buried PVC pipe with a single opening as permanent hideaways. A secure, weighted lid is essential — ribbon eels are capable of escaping through small gaps. They are extremely sensitive to poor water quality; ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate should be kept as close to zero as possible.

The species is protandrous hermaphrodite: individuals are born male (black phase), may transition to female (yellow phase) over time. Color change in captivity has been documented but is not guaranteed. Multiple males can sometimes be housed together in large tanks with individual burrow sites, but competition at feeding time may be vigorous.

Diet transition: begin with live small fish (mollies, guppies); attempt to switch to non-living marine protein (squid, silversides, marine shrimp) using a feeding stick and garlic-scented attractant. Consistent feeding in a low-competition environment is critical.

CROSS-SOURCE DISAGREEMENT NOTES — Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria lists 100 gal (379 L); AquariumSource and BuildYourAquarium list 55–75 gal (208–284 L). Averaged to ~265 L. Reviewer may prefer to use the more conservative 379 L. Temperature minimums ranged from 22.2°C (72°F, LiveAquaria/BuildYourAquarium) to 25.6°C (78°F, AquariumSource) and FishBase wild data 24.6°C; maxes ranged 25.6–28.8°C. Averaged to 23.7–27.5°C. Adult size: AquariumSource gives males 66–102 cm, females up to 129 cm; BuildYourAquarium gives 91–122 cm; FishBase max 130 cm; LiveAquaria implies ~127 cm (50 in). Min averaged to ~78.5 cm, max averaged to ~127 cm. Temperament: LiveAquaria and AquariumSource say semi-aggressive; BuildYourAquarium says aggressive — majority vote semi-aggressive recorded. Reef safety: LiveAquaria lists reef-safe; BuildYourAquarium notes possible coral disturbance near hideaways — recorded as 'with caution'.
breedingNotes: Protandrous hermaphrodites: born male (black phase), capable of transitioning to female (yellow phase). Breeding in captivity has been attempted but is essentially not achieved in home aquaria. Fertilized eggs have been reported in zoological settings but larvae have not been successfully raised past the planktonic stage. The larval phase is likely pelagic and highly specialized. Captive breeding is not a realistic goal for home aquarists.

additional.name: AquariumSource
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperature, salinity, pH, kH, diet, temperament, compatibility, lifespan, and native range. Designated secondary (saltwaterfish.com) returned HTTP 403; Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403; Fishkeeping World returned 404. AquariumSource used as replacement secondary.

additional.name: BuildYourAquarium
additional.notes: Third source used to resolve disagreements on temperament, tank size, and temperature range. Provided breeding notes on protandrous hermaphroditism.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Muraenidae, order Anguilliformes), wild maximum size (130 cm), preferred temperature range (24.6–28.8°C), and native range confirmation.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Ribbon eel)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation, wild lifespan data, and Wikimedia Commons image candidate identification.

fish.breedingNotes: Protandrous hermaphrodite; fertilized eggs documented in zoo settings but larvae not successfully raised. Not achievable in home aquaria.
